skill.name: tailwind-v4-shadcn
skill.description: Set up Tailwind CSS v4 with shadcn/ui for Vite and React projects. The skill provides CSS variable architecture, @theme inline mapping, dark mode templates, and migration guidance for common Tailwind v3 mistakes.
skill.summary: Configure Tailwind CSS v4 and shadcn/ui with production-ready React templates.
skill.author: jezweb

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many hits, but most are false positives from Markdown examples, CSS color syntax, and documentation URLs. The confirmed risk is legitimate setup guidance that runs npm, pnpm, npx, and shadcn commands, including @latest package execution and project file changes. No prompt injection, credential access, exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: External package commands run during setup
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs agents to run package manager commands, including shadcn through npx or pnpm dlx. This is expected for frontend setup, but it executes third-party npm packages and changes the project dependency graph.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit setup steps in Markdown code blocks. They are legitimate for this skill, but they execute network-fetched tooling and should be reviewed before use.

medium_findings.title: Project file mutation is part of the workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill tells agents to create, update, and sometimes remove project configuration files. The file changes are scoped to Tailwind and Vite setup, but users should review diffs before applying them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The setup instructions plainly modify project files and remove a Tailwind v3 config. This is normal for migration guidance, but it can affect existing projects.

low_findings.title: Browser storage is limited to theme preference
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The ThemeProvider template reads and writes localStorage and sessionStorage only for a theme key. No credentials, tokens, or user data are collected or transmitted.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The storage value is constrained to light, dark, or system theme preferences. I found no network call or secret handling near this code.

low_findings.title: Dynamic require appears as a warning example
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: require() appears in documentation as an outdated Tailwind v3 pattern to avoid. The skill recommends the Tailwind v4 @plugin directive instead.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text labels require() as old syntax and instructs users not to use it. There is no executable dynamic require implementation.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are documentation references
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The detected URLs point to official documentation, schema metadata, or a production-tested example. I found no code that sends data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in comments, documentation, or schema fields. No fetch, HTTP client, webhook, or exfiltration pattern was found.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are CSS false positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Weak crypto detections correspond to terms such as hsl() color values and Markdown content. No hashing, encryption, or authentication logic is implemented by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged contexts are CSS color declarations and documentation snippets. I found no cryptographic API usage or security-sensitive hash comparison.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unpinned latest package execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup examples use shadcn@latest through npx or pnpm dlx. This is common setup practice, but it executes the current registry package rather than a pinned version.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The @latest invocations are explicit. The risk is supply-chain drift, not malicious code in this skill.

content.value_statement: Tailwind v4 and shadcn/ui setup often fails because older Tailwind v3 patterns are still common. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a clear setup path with templates and migration checks.
actual_capabilities: Installs and configures Tailwind CSS v4 for Vite React projects.
Adds shadcn/ui setup guidance with v4-compatible components.json settings.
Provides a complete CSS variable and @theme inline architecture.
Includes a React ThemeProvider template for light, dark, and system modes.
Explains Tailwind v3 to v4 migration differences and common fixes.
Documents plugin, animation, typography, and semantic color patterns.
limitations: It targets Vite and React, not Next.js or other frameworks.
It relies on external npm packages and registry availability.
It does not verify every generated component visually.
Existing custom Tailwind themes may need manual migration review.

use_cases.title: Start a new Vite React design system
use_cases.description: Set up Tailwind v4, shadcn/ui, semantic colors, and dark mode from the first commit.

use_cases.title: Fix a broken Tailwind v4 migration
use_cases.description: Replace Tailwind v3 config patterns with CSS-first @theme inline configuration.

use_cases.title: Standardize component styling rules
use_cases.description: Give an AI coding assistant repeatable rules for shadcn/ui colors, plugins, and theme tokens.

prompt_templates.title: Basic setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up Tailwind CSS v4 and shadcn/ui in this Vite React project. Use the skill templates and explain each file changed.

prompt_templates.title: Dark mode setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Add the Tailwind v4 shadcn/ui dark mode pattern. Include ThemeProvider, semantic colors, and a simple theme toggle.

prompt_templates.title: Migration repair
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this project for Tailwind v3 patterns and migrate them to Tailwind v4 using @theme inline and the Vite plugin.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced troubleshooting
prompt_templates.prompt: Debug why shadcn/ui colors and dark mode are not working. Check components.json, CSS variables, @theme inline mapping, and plugin syntax.

output_examples.input: Set up Tailwind v4 and shadcn/ui in my Vite React app.
output_examples.output: The assistant installs the needed packages, updates Vite configuration, creates CSS theme tokens, adds shadcn/ui settings, and summarizes changed files.

output_examples.input: My bg-primary class does not work after migration.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks for missing @theme inline mappings, verifies CSS variables, and explains why semantic utilities were not generated.

output_examples.input: Add dark mode with system preference support.
output_examples.output: The assistant adds a theme provider, stores only the theme preference, updates root classes, and verifies semantic colors switch correctly.

best_practices: Review package install commands before running them in production repositories.
Keep Tailwind v4 theme tokens in CSS and map them with @theme inline.
Test light, dark, and system theme modes after setup changes.
anti_patterns: Do not use Tailwind v3 config files as the source of truth for v4 themes.
Do not use raw color classes when semantic shadcn/ui tokens are required.
Do not run shadcn@latest in locked-down projects without dependency review.

faq.question: Does this skill support Tailwind CSS v3?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for Tailwind CSS v4 and warns against v3-only patterns.

faq.question: Does it work with Next.js?
faq.answer: Not directly. The guidance targets Vite and React projects.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention external commands?
faq.answer: The setup requires npm, pnpm, npx, and shadcn commands. Review them before execution.

faq.question: Does the theme provider store sensitive data?
faq.answer: No. The template stores only a theme preference such as light, dark, or system.

faq.question: What problem does @theme inline solve?
faq.answer: It maps CSS variables to Tailwind utilities so classes like bg-background and text-primary work.

faq.question: Can Claude Code and Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
